item.title: The Garden of Earthly Delights
item.lesson: Read it like a comic strip of the cosmos: Eden on the left, where God presents Eve to Adam; a teeming pink-and-blue carnival of naked humanity in the center, feasting on giant berries and riding oversized birds; and on the right, a night-black hell where music is torture and a tree-man with a broken eggshell body gazes back at us. Nobody knows what Bosch meant. Is the center panel a warning about lust, or a vision of a world that never fell? The painting's first documented owners hung it in a palace, and Philip II of Spain — the most militantly Catholic king in Europe — kept it at his monastery-palace of El Escorial, which suggests it read as moral instruction. Five centuries later, the Surrealists claimed Bosch as their ancestor: a painter of dreams four hundred years before anyone had the word for it.
item.lookFor: In the hell panel, find the pair of ears pierced by an arrow, wielding a knife blade — one of art's strangest inventions, marching across the darkness like a war machine.
